narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your outstanding undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 39, which exceeds the usual 36.70 mark, along with your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 73.8% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you at the lower end compared to the average of around the 6th decile, which had the strongest negative influence overall. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the typical 6th decile, was the second-most influential factor pushing in the same concerning direction. On a brighter note, your high undergraduate GPA strongly countered this by nudging the prediction toward success, while your LSAT score provided a moderate positive push as well. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early academic deciles stand out as unusually low, overshadowing your later achievements and demographics that align more closely with averages. Your full-time status and family income had minimal sway, neither helping nor hurting much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school decile rankings, as those proved decisive here, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You have the GPA and LSAT foundation to succeed—keep pushing, because your story deserves to be told in the courtroom. We're rooting for you.
